full_text:
Astma case study

Introduction
Asthma is a major non-communicable disease that affects both adults and children worldwide; in extreme situations, it can lead to substantial morbidity and mortality (Dharmage et al., 2019). It is currently the most prevalent chronic illness in children and is becoming more widespread globally (Kelada et al., 2021). Parents have commonly admitted the fact that they are often uncertain of the appropriate action to take due to the lack of understanding associated with the deterioration of their children during asthmatic crises (Shulhan-Kilroy et al., 2022). To this end, a need for clearer education on how to manage exacerbation arises (Shulhan-Kilroy et al., 2022). In response to the rising prevalence, the education deficit and the complex nature of paediatric asthma, the Irish paediatrics services now focus on proactive interventions that involve members of the community. This transition is clearly backed by government policy and strategies such as the Sláintecare Implementation Strategy (Government of Ireland, 2018), which mandates the relocation of chronic disease management into the community through education, and the Health Service Executive's (Health Service Executive, 2021) Framework for Self-management Support.
The role of the children’s nurse is underpinned by child- and family-centred care and borders around partnership, communication, and respect for the child’s autonomy. According to Seniwati et al. (2023), this role involves the integration of physical, emotional, and psychosocial dimensions of care. It is one that allows the child to participate in practice (Carter et al., 2024). However, Bawah et al. (2023) argue that despite the emphasis placed on this by existing policies, meaningful involvement of children in decision-making remains inconsistent. In the management of acute asthma, the role of a children’s nurse extends even further to clinical interventions that stabilise the patient and actions that promote family engagement. And such interventions may include the administration of oxygen therapy and bronchodilators (Martin et al., 2022; Indinnimeo et al., 2018).
Working within the Nursing and Midwifery Board of Ireland Scope of practice (Nursing and Midwifery Board of Ireland, 2015), this case study aims to critically evaluate a case of acute asthma. Daniel, the pseudonym adopted for this case study, is an 8-year-old boy who was admitted to the hospital where I worked as a student nurse; he presented with an acute asthma exacerbation that occurred after playing outside, and is unresponsive to home bronchodilator therapy. His acute respiratory distress is characterised by tachypnoea, hypoxia, and use of accessory muscles for breathing. In my interactions with him and his mother, priority was placed on a clear explanation of the situation, reassurance and shared teaching to reduce anxiety which resulted from the sudden physiological deterioration of the child (Volerman et al., 2019).
The essay will proceed with the critical analysis of the situational variables during Daniel's admission. It will evaluate the acute respiratory assessment via the Paediatric Early Warning Score (PEWS), the pharmacological management of his exacerbation, and the indispensable role of family-centred discharge education. Finally, a conclusion will summarise key findings and provide a critical reflection on my professional development.


Respiratory Assessment and the Role of PEWS
Upon admission, given the urgency of the situation, immediate clinical assessment was necessitated. The nursing assessment followed the ABCDE (Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure) framework to discover the respiratory compromise in Daniel, which was characterised by severe tachypnea, hypoxia, the pronounced use of accessory musculature and a widespread expiratory wheeze noted upon auscultation (Bruinink et al., 2024; Joseph & Ganatra, 2024). The compensatory use of accessory muscles, as Panetti et al. (2024) posit, is a critical mechanism invoked to overcome hyperinflation and severe airway limitations, acting often as a precursor to life-threatening respiratory fatigue. Tachypnoea, on the other hand, precedes a precipitous drop in recorded oxygen saturation (SpO₂), thus acting as a sign of pending systemic collapse (Smith & Bowden, 2017). During assessment, his oxygen saturation (SpO₂) was found to be below normal despite the effort of his accessory respiratory muscles, a sign of existing fatigue, potential collapse and a prompt for intervention. 
Sometimes, due to a higher physiological reserve compared to adults, children in Daniel’s condition present with an SpO₂ that is normal in value (Lambert et al., 2017). Should a nurse tether their clinical judgement exclusively to this reading, the severity of the emergency may be misunderstood. It is in recognition of this risk, and in a bid to standardise the system with which physiological deterioration in respiratory distress is recognised, that the Irish National Clinical Guideline No. 12 mandates the use of the Paediatric Early Warning System (PEWS) (Department of Health, 2016). The system is designed to create an alert that notifies the clinician following the event of clinical deterioration in children by periodic observation of their physiological parameters and the generation of a score based on predetermined criteria, to prompt urgent assistance with a clear framework for communication and escalation (Lambert et al., 2017). However, the infallibility of the PEWS system remains the subject of intense critical debate. Proponents, such as Lambert et al. (2017), argue that the system is an indispensable sociotechnical intervention, despite the lack of empirical data that ties the tool to child mortality; given that the latter is a rare clinical event. They contend that PEWS reduces the existing hierarchical barriers to communication and empowers junior nurses to escalate care confidently when necessary. On the other hand, this perceived utility is contested by Trubey et al. (2019), whose comprehensive systematic review noted that track-and-trigger tools like the PEWS are unreliable. This is because the tool produces low positive predictive values and is susceptible to false alarms (Trubey et al., 2019). The research by Trubey et al. (2019) can be seen as valid for several reasons. It was conducted in accordance with PRISMA guidelines across ten major healthcare databases and this ensured its reproducibility (Page et al., 2021). Included studies were independently assessed for quality using a modified Downs and Black rating scale, meaning that differences in study quality were systematically accounted for rather than left to subjective judgement (Mheissen et al., 2024). However, heterogeneity across studies undermined the accuracy of any pooled synthesis and weakened its conclusion (Mheissen et al., 2024). Additionally, it contains studies about effectiveness of the tool which were uncontrolled before-after by design, and this limited causality (Digitale et al., 2021). But, Jacob et al. (2019) adopt a middle ground by concluding that the danger resides not within the tool itself but in the cognitive complacency its implementation can induce. According to them, an over-reliance on numerical thresholds is capable of fostering a clinical culture that is reactive, wherein practitioners treat their patients solely by the parameters on the chart, without including insights from their professional intuition and the unquantifiable family concern into the decision-making framework. Therefore, It becomes evident upon evaluation of these contrasting views that while PEWS provided the necessary objective data required to expedite Daniel's medical review, it should not be used at face value and in isolation. Indeed, during the provision of care, this tool was indispensable yet did not replace my clinical intuition. Instead, it provided objective information upon which I escalated his condition to the supervising senior nurse, and based on which his pharmacological management in accordance with Children's Health Ireland (CHI) clinical guideline was initiated (Children’s Health Ireland, 2021).
Nursing Intervention: Positioning, Oxygen and Bronchodilator Therapy
After assessment, Daniel’s care proceeded with a repositioning to an upright position so as to reduce the distress and support ventilation. Following this was the administration of high-flow oxygen to raise his oxygen saturation level. He was also administered nebulised bronchodilators (salbutamol).
According to Dünser et al. (2018), upright, forward-leaning positioning maximises respiratory muscle contraction, while Morrow (2019) identifies that such posture improves end-expiratory lung volumes and in the process prevents the premature closure of the airways. Furthermore, Petrocheilou et al. (2020) assert that an upright posture exposes enough surface area for aerosolised medications to act during nebulisation. This is also supported by the Children's Health Ireland guideline that indicates the rapid administration of β2-agonists, salbutamol in Daniel’s case, along with oxygen to help reverse the acute bronchospasm and hypoxia (Children’s Health Ireland, 2021; Leung, 2021). But the clinical efficacy of nebulised salbutamol is not absolute. According to Dentice et al. (2019), a typical jet nebuliser only deposits between 11% and 16% of the loaded dose into the lungs. Also, aggressive doses of β2-agonists do not yield a linear therapeutic benefit; when given in excess of their pharmacological threshold, Salbutamol-Induced Lactic Acidosis (SILA) precipitates (Pearmain et al., 2020). As a consequence, compensatory tachypnoea may result (Sharif & Al-Alawi, 2018). For this reason, Daniel was monitored closely. Furthermore, asthmatic children and patients in a similar situation as Daniel, require an immediate supplementation of oxygen to levels greater than 94% saturation (Chavasse & Scott, 2019). Oxygen administration, however, should not be given in an uncontrolled manner, as Kostakou et al. (2019) maintain that such action for untitrated, high-flow oxygen may result in hyperoxia, which in turn masks the ongoing respiratory deterioration. Consequently, high concentration of oxygen was administered rapidly in the initial phase to improve Daniel’s saturation, but the therapy was subsequently titrated to maintain levels within safe clinical parameters (94-98%). 
Family-Centred Care and Discharge Education in Paediatric Asthma
Family-centred care is defined as a different and unique approach to the planning of health care interventions, their delivery and the evaluation of results (Chow et al., 2024). This type of care involves parents during the assessment of their child’s condition, at various points of decision-making and also during the course of treatment and healthcare (Kemble et al., 2024). Its components include information sharing, respect, participation, and collaboration; collectively, these make families active participants in the care of their loved ones (Seniwati et al., 2023). It is very important in certain areas of healthcare, more so in paediatric asthma, where the management of the disease condition must extend beyond the hospital environment into communities and households (Liu et al., 2022). According to Shulhan-Kilroy et al. (2022), clear parental understanding of the symptoms of a disease and how to identify them, sufficient knowledge of the triggers and how to avoid them and medication use influence to a great extent outcomes such as adherence and rates of re-admission. Hence, it is imperative for nurses to assess, in addition to providing health information, the knowledge base of parents with regard to the disease, their emotional readiness, and capacity to manage care at home. This is in line with the findings of Kemble et al. (2024), who emphasise that the tailored provision of nurse-led education enhances self-management and empowers families to respond appropriately to exacerbations. 
But despite its recognised benefits, the implementation of family-centred care is frequently constrained in acute settings. According to O’Connor et al. (2019), time pressures and clinical urgency can reorient clinical interactions to task-orientated communication, which would most probably undermine the development of therapeutic relationships. Furthermore, during acute deterioration of a patient in emergency service provision, clinicians often regress into authoritarian roles by instinct and act in ways restricted by their core biomedical knowledge of the disease with little or no input from the family (Hill et al., 2019; Phiri et al., 2019). If allowed to persist, a lack of family-centred care, like Shulhan-Kilroy et al. (2022) posits, will lead to a situation where parents feel an increased level of anxiety, less confidence in the management of their child’s condition and a high risk of delayed or unnecessary presentation of their child to such hospitals for emergency services.
In Daniel’s case, family-centred care was ensured through clear explanation of interventions, reassurance during acute deterioration, and education on inhaler technique and trigger management. The approach yielded positive results seen in the immediate relief and reduction of anxiety in the mother and a boost in her confidence and competence to manage the condition at home. It was pertinent that Daniel’s parents, in this case his mother, understood how to administer his medications at home to guarantee continuity of care in the community. Without this, prescription of maintenance medications is futile, as the inhaler drugs may be compromised by poor use of spacers. In this respect, Cook et al. (2017) identified mechanical errors from the improper use of these spacers as the primary catalyst that can hasten the deterioration from a simple childhood wheeze to refractory asthma, which is often problematic. Similarly, Glick et al. (2017) highlight in their systematic review the nature of the hospital discharge process as one that overwhelms parental memory and results in higher rates of medication non-adherence and dosing errors. By noting the anxiety in Daniel’s mother, albeit reduced, it was clinically apparent that a traditional, passive verbal handover would be inadequate. Hence, with my experience while being an intellectual disability nurse, I broke the process of using the inhaler down into discrete and tactile components and then described it to her using a stepwise teaching approach. This method is consistent with that adopted in specialist intellectual-disability nursing, where emphasis is placed on person-centred care, health education, and the use of specialised communication to involve patients and their relatives in care and decision-making (Doody et al., 2023). After this process, the patient was required to physically demonstrate the assembly and administration technique she had just learnt. This exercise was informed by the show-back methodology as described by Kennedy et al. (2022), who found it to be very effective. Also, it is validated by findings from Yeung et al. (2018), who found that the written Asthma Action Plan given to parents without clear explanations and the inculcation of the mechanical skills needed to execute the prescribed interventions led to an increase in the number of re-admissions in the emergency department for the case.
While Asthma Action Plans are unequivocally championed as the fulcrum of paediatric self-management, Simon and Akinbami (2016) in their study note that nearly half of all asthmatic children never receive one. Yet, even when a plan is issued, the lack of awareness of schools and other institutions that the child interacts with in their daily life creates a gap in its implementation (Volerman et al., 2019). As a consequence, carers are forced to find alternative means which can be as bad as retaining children at home or visiting the schools to administer midday bronchodilators (Volerman et al., 2019). For Daniel, whose acute deterioration followed an outdoor activity (playing), restricting his discharge education exclusively to the domestic environment may trigger readmission when he engages in a similar activity in school or other outdoor locations. To mitigate this, Trivedi et al. (2018) posit that formalised clinical-community linkages forged through school-nurse-supervised therapeutic programmes lead to a significant reduction in such emergencies. Hence the reason why Daniel’s physical discharge from the ward was with an asthma action plan that includes instructions on how to coordinate with authorities and implement it in his school and other outdoor environments in the community.
Conclusion and Reflection
This essay critically evaluated Daniel’s case, an 8-year-old boy who was admitted for acute asthma exacerbation that occurred after playing outside. Daniel’s presentation demonstrated the importance of ABCDE assessment and the Paediatric Early Warning System in the recognition and assessment of respiratory deterioration and also the need to interpret readings from these tools alongside clinical judgement. Upright positioning, titrated oxygen therapy, and nebulised salbutamol, all of which were nursing interventions executed during his care, were of great value and played a role in relieving hypoxia and bronchospasm. These interventions, however, require vigilance to ensure the medications are delivered within safe clinical limits to mitigate adverse outcomes like the accumulation of salbutamol-induced lactic acidosis and hyperoxia. But Daniel’s healthcare is incomplete without a structured, family-centred discharge education. Hence, for this reason, an asthma action plan that involved players from his school and community was developed and discussed with the mother. This was done to reduce the risk of readmission.
As one of my first experiences in paediatrics nursing, as a person who was previously an intellectual disability nurse, this presented me with emotional and psychological disturbances. Witnessing Daniel’s respiratory deterioration, I felt a deep sense of pity and sadness. The urgency of the situation required an initial execution of a series of biomedical tasks in a bid to stabilise him. But seeing his mother anxious and worried about the fate of her son left a deep sense of awareness of the importance of carrying family members along in situations like this. And when applied, I learnt its importance (family-centred care) and how effective it is in bringing calm to patient relatives in the course of treatment. Furthermore, the application of the show-back methodology in the process of teaching the patient’s mother how best to apply his inhaler medication was a step in the right direction.
Now, with more knowledge and wisdom, I recognise that the total regression to biomedical tasks in an authoritarian manner during the emergency was capable of sabotaging the development of therapeutic partnerships. By this action, I failed to provide ‘partnership in care’ at an early stage, which is a core tenet of children’s nursing scope of practice. Furthermore, my failure to support Daniel’s autonomy during the crisis is another point of error. Prioritising his mother’s anxiety and consent led to a dismissal of his participation.
This reflection has clearly delineated my future learning needs; hence, going forward, I am now committed to learning more about paediatrics nursing care to ensure a complete transition from my previous professional identity as an intellectual disability nurse, albeit with retained experience. Also, I will improve my communication and advocacy skills to ensure seamless interaction with patient relatives during the discharge of family-centred care. Finally, I must ensure that communication styles and modes appropriate for patients of Daniel’s age in the future will be used to explain and involve the child in making simple decisions about the care they are receiving.
